data.title: The Social Security Trust Fund Is Rapidly Approaching Insolvency
data.description:
Introduction

I’m old enough to remember when Al Gore coined the term “lockbox” as a euphemism for how he was going to protect the Social Security surplus.

The year was 2000, and Gore was the incumbent Vice President, campaigning for the presidency. His administration had just recorded a budget surplus of $236 billion, the largest in American history still to this day. More than two-thirds of the surplus, or $160 billion, was created by the revenue from Social Security taxes exceeding payments to Social Security recipients.

Vice President Gore wanted to save that Social Security surplus for the future, while his opponent, then Texas Governor George W. Bush, wanted to return the surplus to American citizens through tax cuts.

Gore was memorably parodied by Darrell Hammond on Saturday Night Live, a performance that helped turn the word “lockbox” into a lasting piece of SNL lore.

Unfortunately, American retirees will not be laughing now.

CBO Forecasts That The Social Security Surplus Will Be Depleted By The End Of Fiscal Year 2031

On Wednesday, Congressional Budget Office Director Phillip Swagel testified before the Senate Finance Committee about the fiscal outlook over the next ten years. As he has for the past several years, Swagel stressed how our expenditures continue to exceed our revenues, causing our debt to GDP to balloon. He warned the lawmakers, again, that the trajectory of budget deficits was unsustainable.

Of particular interest to me, though, was Swagel emphasizing that Social Security’s Old Age and Survivors Insurance (OASI) Trust Fund would be exhausted by the end of fiscal year 2031.

That is only 5 years away!

As recently as 2022, when CBO published a similar ten-year fiscal outlook, the forecast was that the Trust Fund would last until 2035.

Even one year ago, CBO projected that OASI wouldn’t become insolvent until the end of Fiscal Year 2032.

Over the past 4 years, the expected end of OASI has been shortened by four years. This is an extremely rapid deterioration in the Social Security program.

Why Social Security Is Deteriorating

The Social Security Act was passed in 1935, during the Great Depression, as part of President Franklin D. Roosevelt’s New Deal. There was widespread unemployment and poverty in our country, and the Act was created as a contributory system that would provide future retirement benefits for workers.

The Old Age and Survivors Insurance Trust Fund was established in 1939 as the depository of all Social Security payroll taxes collected by the government. After benefits were paid, the surplus was invested in non-marketable U.S. Government bonds.

Everyone who ever received a paystub has been surprised to see that their take-home pay was much less than the gross amount they earned for the pay period. They knew that they would have state and federal income taxes withheld from their check, but then there was this mysterious FICA tax as well.

The Federal Insurance Contributions Act, or FICA, is a mandatory federal payroll tax deduction of 6.2% of earnings to fund Social Security. This amount is matched by employers.

There have been many changes to Social Security over the years to extend its solvency. The Social Security Amendment of 1983 accelerated payroll tax increases and gradually raised the full retirement age from 65 to 67. This led to a significant increase in the Social Security surplus.

The fundamental problem with Social Security, though, is a demographic one. We have an aging population, and the ratio of workers paying taxes to retirees receiving benefits is shrinking. In 1940, shortly after the program was created, there were many workers and few retirees who had paid into the system. The ratio started at 159 to 1.

As the program matured, the ratio narrowed. By 1960 the ratio was 5.3 to 1, and gradually it has declined to the current ratio of 2.8 to 1. By 2035 it is expected to fall further to 2.2 to 1.

The impact of this demographic shift is that current Social Security revenue is no longer large enough to cover current Social Security expenses. Hence, to meet the shortfall to pay retirees their earned benefits, Social Security must dip into the OASI Trust Fund.

Annual Social Security expenses began exceeding annual revenues in 2021. This was in part due to an acceleration in Cost-of-Living Adjustments tied to the then 40-year high CPI increase. That the Fed has not been able to bring CPI back down to its target 2% rate has exacerbated the problem.

Last summer’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act increased the deficit further by reducing total revenue targeted for Social Security.

As seen in the above chart, the annual Social Security deficit is growing rapidly; hence, it needs to reach for the Trust Fund to cover the shortfall. But, as stated earlier, the Trust Fund will only be able to cover Social Security benefits for 5 more years.

What This Means For Retirees

Americans depend on, and rely on, Social Security. Currently there are almost 70 million retirees collecting Social Security benefits. Of these people, almost half receive at least 50% of their household income from Social Security, and 25% count on Social Security for 90% of their income.

In fiscal year 2032, if nothing is done, OASI will become insolvent, and retirees will see an automatic drastic cut in their Social Security benefits. The agency will only have current income to meet expenses,

The Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget estimates that in this situation, benefits will be cut by 24% across the board. Such an action would be devastating to the neediest of families, leading many to sink deeper into poverty.

There would be a ripple effect throughout the economy. Consumer spending would drop, and growth would slow. Plus, strains would be placed on other government-funded social service programs, both federal and state.

It is simply an untenable situation.

What Must Be Done

This is a five-alarm fire situation that has significant long-term costs for our country. Yet, it is not well known.

Yes, everyone sees that Social Security is the single largest program in the federal budget, accounting for 22% of total federal spending, but the severity of the problem has not been widely discussed. And time is of the essence.

What should be done is something similar to what was done the last time Social Security expenses exceeded Social Security revenues on an annual basis. That period occurred in the late 1970s. When President Ronald Reagan came into office in 1981, he created the National Commission on Social Security Reform. This was a 15-member bipartisan group tasked with solving the severe pending crisis with Social Security.

The negotiations were difficult and tense, but ultimately a compromise was reached. The Social Security Act of 1983 was passed and has sustained Social Security for the past 43 years. The time is now for an update.

Conclusion

CBO Director Phillip Swagel testified before the Senate Finance Committee regarding the fiscal outlook over the next 10 years, and what stood out to me was the pending insolvency of the Social Security Trust Fund by the end of fiscal year 2031.

In 2021, annual Social Security expenses began exceeding revenue, requiring dipping into the OASI Trust Fund to cover the shortfall. This operating deficit will continue to grow based on CBO projections, and the Trust Fund will become insolvent in the next 5 years.

If nothing is done, Social Security recipients will see their benefits slashed by 24% across the board, according to the Committee for a Responsible Budget.

This will have a devastating impact on the 70 million Americans who count on Social Security. Benefits currently represent 50% of household income for almost half of retirees, and 25% of retirees count on Social Security for 90% of their income

A drastic cut to benefits will send many to deep poverty, cause consumer spending to slow, weaken the economy, and put a strain on other state and federal social service agencies.

Something needs to be done, on a bipartisan basis, to solve this imminent Social Security crisis.

Where is the lockbox when we need it?

data.title: Myers Industries: Growth In Industrial And Infrastructure Supports Margins, But Premium Valuation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Myers Industries, Inc. (MYE) is expected to drive revenue growth from end markets such as Industrial and Infrastructure. However, the Food & Beverage end market should be a headwind to net sales in the coming quarters. On the profitability front, the cost-saving program and favorable mix should be accretive to margins in FY26. Based on my relative model, I believe the stock is trading at a premium of ~19%. Although I like the growth prospect of the company, I believe the forward earnings growth is already priced into the current valuation.

Business Overview

Myers Industries is a manufacturer of polymer products and material handling solutions based in the United States. The company engages in the production of plastic containers, pallets, fuel containers, and protective packaging, which are used in various end markets, including Infrastructure, Industrial, Vehicles, Food & Beverage, and Consumer. The products of the company help in supporting storage, transportation, and logistics in supply chains. The company primarily operates in North America and focuses on higher-margin growth products.

Revenue Analysis & Outlook

In FY2025, the net sales were $825.7 million, a decrease of 1.3% year-over-year or down 0.6% year-over-year excluding the impact from exiting the lower-margin products. The decline was due to the weakness in the distribution segment. However, the growth from the Material Handling segment provided support to net sales in FY2025. In the Material Handling segment, the net sales from Industrial and Infrastructure end markets were up year-over-year. But the sales were down year-over-year from the consumer and Vehicle end markets.

Looking ahead, I believe the Industrial and Infrastructure end markets should support the revenue in the coming quarters. However, the Food & Beverage end market should be a headwind in revenue growth. Moreover, the Vehicle end market is expected to remain stable in the future. The revenue should be impacted by the exit of rotomolded plastic products. In the recent quarter, the company discontinued the lower-margin products, particularly rotomolded plastic products, and idled two facilities in Ohio. This implies a net sales loss of $5 million per quarter or $20 million in the fiscal year.

The overall demand in the Industrial end market of the company is linked with manufacturing activity. The US Manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) is a leading indicator of manufacturing activity in the country. PMI was at 51.6 points in February 2026, above the benchmark of 50.0 points. But, down from the January PMI data. The February PMI was supported by increased output and new orders. However, the demand was impacted by higher prices, tariff impacts, and bad weather conditions. Despite weak momentum in February, business owner sentiments were strengthened, driven by plans of business expansion and the launch of new products. Additionally, rising geopolitical tension and military conflicts should increase the defense budgets, particularly among the US and NATO countries. This should increase the demand for the defense products of the company in the coming quarters. Although the Industrial end market is largely cyclical, I believe the improving manufacturing activity, coupled with increasing defense demand, should provide support for the revenue from this segment.

The growth in the Infrastructure end market should remain strong, driven by robust spending on Infrastructure and an increasing addressable market. In the recent earnings call, management mentioned increasing demand from the growth in hyperscale projects and data center construction. Hyperscaler projects are the large-scale infrastructure projects to support cloud computing, AI workloads, and other services. The hyperscaler projects are created by the Big Five, including Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL) or Google, Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and Oracle Corporation (ORCL). The capex by the Big Five hyperscalers is expected to exceed $600 billion in 2026, an increase of 36% year-over-year. This should be driven by increased investments in AI infrastructure and data center expansion. Additionally, there is an ongoing shift in the market from the traditional wood matting to durable composite matting, which should increase the Total Addressable Market (TAM) of Myers' products. Currently, the backlog for the matting products is at a record level, and the backlog-to-sales conversion provides strong momentum entering FY2026.

The Vehicle end market of the company deals in recreational vehicles, marine, and automotive component packaging. The demand for recreational vehicles and marine remains subdued due to the lower consumer spending in the market. A higher interest rate environment led to lower spending on discretionary purchases by customers. Although the Federal Reserve has implemented rate cuts previously, the long-term borrowing costs are still elevated. At present, the U.S. 30 Year Treasury Yield is at 4.8%, up ~ 20 basis points year-over-year. This could impact the financing of discretionary purchases like recreational vehicles and marine products. However, the management believes that the market should be stabilized gradually with the improving consumer confidence.

The Food & Beverage end market of the company engages in agricultural storage solutions, which are used to transport and store crops. The demand in this end market is influenced by the agricultural industry cycle. Currently, the agricultural industry is operating at the lower end of its cycle due to weak commodity prices and lower farmer spending. This should weigh on the Myers’ revenue in the near term. However, as agricultural conditions improve, Myers, with its important role in agricultural logistics through seed distribution and bulk material handling, should deliver good results in the long term.

Margin Analysis & Outlook

In FY2025, the adjusted gross margin stood at 33.7%, an increase of 30 basis points from the previous year, supported by the lower material costs, favorable cost productivity, and favorable sales mix. Moreover, the adjusted operating margin was at 10.3%, an improvement of ~30 basis points year-over-year, driven by the cost-saving benefits from the Focus transformation program.

The margins of the company should improve in FY2026, supported by the cost savings from the Focus transformation program, a favorable revenue mix, and improved facility utilization through exiting lower-margin products. However, the potential headwinds should be pricing dynamics, higher input costs (particularly resin), and operational deleverage from the weakness in end markets like Food & Beverage and Vehicle.

The continued efforts on the cost-saving program, i.e., the Focus transformation program, have already helped the company in generating approximately ~$20 million in annualized cost savings. The program includes structural Selling, General, & Administration (SG&A) cost reduction and efficiency initiatives. Moreover, the company has discontinued the production of low-margin products and exited two rotational molding facilities, which should improve the cost efficiency and enhance the manufacturing utilization across the remaining footprint. Additionally, the company is also focusing on higher-margin products growth, such as infrastructure matting, industrial solutions, and other defense products.

Despite these improvements, some factors should weigh over margins in the coming quarters. Resin is the main input for plastic manufacturing, which is expected to remain elevated. It is expected to negatively impact the margins if the cost increases are not fully offset by the pricing actions and productivity gains. Pricing in the market is dynamic as the higher input costs are pressuring the manufacturers to increase the price. But the highly competitive landscape is limiting the company’s ability to pass on increased input costs to customers. Additionally, the demand in the end markets, such as Food & Beverage and vehicles, is expected to be weak, which should limit volume growth and operational leverage.

Valuation

Myers Industries is currently trading at a non-GAAP P/E (fwd) of 16.69x with a consensus EPS estimate of $1.26. The major end markets, including Industrial and Infrastructure, are expected to support the revenue in the coming quarters. Moreover, the other end markets, like Vehicles, are expected to stabilize in the future. However, the Food & Beverage end market should be challenging due to lower demand in the agriculture industry. With key end markets expected to remain healthy, I believe the company should report increased revenue on year-over-year basis in FY2026. On the profitability front, the cost-saving programs and favorable revenue mix should be accretive to the overall margins. But the margin expansion depends on pressure from the high input price and pricing actions. Furthermore, I have created a relative valuation model to compare the company along with its peers based on revenue growth rate, operating margin, and P/E ratios. Looking at the revenue growth outlook, UFP Industries is expected to decline by 0.13%, which is lower than the mean of the peer forward revenue growth rate of about 3.19%. Moreover, on the profitability front, the operating margin (TTM) of the company stood at 10.33%, which is more than the peer median margin of approximately 6.65. Comparing Myers’ P/E ratio of 16.69x with its peers, it is above the median P/E ratio of 14.00x.

Risk

My investment thesis assumes moderate growth from the Industrial end market. However, if the macroeconomic conditions deteriorate, it could negatively impact the results from the industrial end market of the company. Additionally, if the high-interest rate environment continues, it could further pressure the discretionary spending of consumers. This may pose a headwind for sales in the Vehicle end market. The impact of price/cost dynamics is uncertain as the material input costs increase, but the company is reluctant to implement price hikes due to increased competition. This uncertainty could weigh on the company's results more than expected.

Conclusion

While it is true that the majority of the company’s end markets, including Infrastructure and Industrial, are expected to support the revenue, I believe the macroeconomic uncertainty may weigh on the near-term performance. Moreover, the pricing power is limited by a highly competitive landscape, and pressure remains from high-input costs. Based on consensus EPS estimates and a 5-year average FWD P/E ratio of 15.04x, the company is trading at a premium of approximately 11%. Moreover, compared to the sector median P/E ratio, the company is at a ~19% premium despite Myers’ lowest forward revenue growth among its peers. Therefore, I believe investors should wait for a better entry point and assign a Hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Invesco Rising Dividends Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Activity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Invesco Rising Dividends Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Portfolio positioning

Compared to the Russell 1000 Index, the fund is generally balanced across sectors and industries, with exposure to areas that have been benefiting from long-term secular growth tailwinds, including e-commerce, electric vehicles, cloud computing, industrial automation, medical technology and broadband. Importantly, our dual focus on companies generating sustainable levels of free cash flow and having healthy balance sheets gives the fund a quality bias and provides defensive characteristics that we believe should prove valuable if market volatility persists.

Notable Additions

Linde (LIN) was purchased due to its essential and resilient business model, anchored by long-term contracts and leadership in industrial gases, which we believe positions it to benefit from megatrends like global energy transition and technology growth.

Abbott Laboratories (ABT) was added to the fund due to its diversified medical technology leadership, robust new product pipeline, including the upcoming Libre CGM sensor partnership with Medtronic (not a fund holding), and a favorable legal position in infant formula litigation. Despite near-term headwinds, we believe Abbott’s solid fundamentals and growth prospects position it well for long-term value.

Republic Services (RSG) is one of the largest US waste management companies and was added to the fund due to what we saw as its attractive risk/reward profile, solid execution and rising consensus estimates. With its consolidated industry structure, high barriers to entry, strong recurring revenues and compelling free cash flow yield, we believe Republic Services offers potential for stable growth, dividend increases and share repurchases, making it a defensive, value-oriented holding.

Notable Sales

Oracle (ORCL) is a major US technology company providing database software, cloud infrastructure and enterprise applications. We sold it due to concerns about downside risk as its investment in AI infrastructure has caused rising debt and negative free cash flow.

Stryker (SYK), a medical technology company, was sold because we believed the risk/reward profile had become less attractive as its valuation rose.

Smurfit Westrock (SW), a packaging company, was sold due to deteriorating fundamentals that have seemingly led to negative investor sentiment and uncertain timing for the stock to recover despite what we consider an attractive free cash flow yield.

Performance highlights

The fund delivered a positive return and performed in line with the Russell 1000 Index during the quarter. Strong stock selection in the communication services, financials, industrials and real estate sectors added the most to relative performance.

Stock selection in the information technology, health care, energy and utilities sectors detracted from relative results.

Contributors to performance

Alphabet reported strong quarterly results, beating expectations across all key metrics, with accelerated growth in Search, YouTube and Google Cloud. Google Cloud’s backlog surged 82% year over year, driving improved operating margins.

Eli Lilly’s stock surged during the quarter, driven by strong momentum in its GLP-1 obesity drug franchise, widespread analyst upgrades, strategic product pipeline partnerships and speculation about acquisitions, culminating in the company reaching a $1 trillion market capitalization. These factors, combined with robust earnings and apparent optimism toward future therapies, propelled the stock higher.

Apple’s stock rally was driven by record iPhone 17 sales, all-time high services revenue, profit margin expansion, earnings that beat expectations and upbeat management guidance, which appeared to fuel investor enthusiasm about AI, customer loyalty and the company’s resilient ecosystem.

Detractors from performance

Microsoft reported strong revenue and earnings that beat expectations. However, the stock lagged during the quarter, apparently due to supply constraints amid stronger-than-anticipated demand for data center capacity that limited near-term upside. Growth in Azure and commercial cloud bookings remained robust, but the supply-demand imbalance is expected to persist through the fiscal year.

Oracle’s stock underperformed due to concerns about its heavy AI infrastructure spending, amid surging capital expenditures and upward revisions to annual spending forecasts. Despite a large backlog of AI and cloud contracts, doubts about the timing of revenue realization and potential margin and debt pressures from elevated spending led to stock volatility and a sharp pullback.

Meta delivered strong revenue growth but the stock price lagged amid concerns about higher AI-related capital expenditures and operating expenses, significant losses in Reality Labs and a large one-time tax charge that pressured earnings. These factors appeared to raise fears about near-term profitability and free cash flow, seemingly overshadowing growth momentum and leading to cautious guidance.

* Alpha (cash adjusted) is a measure of performance on a risk-adjusted basis. Beta (cash adjusted) is a measure of relative risk and the slope of regression. Sharpe Ratio is a risk-adjusted measure calculated using standard deviation and excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. A higher Sharpe ratio indicates better risk-adjusted performance. Information Ratio is a measurement of portfolio returns beyond the returns of a benchmark, usually an index, compared to the volatility of those returns. Standard deviation measures a fund's range of total returns and identifies the spread of a fund's short-term fluctuations. Tracking Error is defined as the expected standard deviation of a portfolio's excess return over the benchmark index return. The up and down capture measures how well a manager was able to replicate or improve on periods of positive benchmark returns and how severely the manager was affected by periods of negative benchmark returns. Maximum Drawdown is the maximum observed loss from a high to a low of a portfolio, before a new high is attained. Maximum drawdown is an indicator of downside risk over a specified time period. Weighted Average Market Cap is a measure of the average size of company held in a portfolio. The percentage of the portfolio invested each company, or its weight, is multiplied by its size (market capitalization). An average of the weighted size of all companies held is then calculated. Price/earnings measures the price per share relative to the earnings per share of the company while excluding extraordinary items. Price to book measures the firm's capitalization (market price) to book value. Est. 3-5 year EPS (Earning per share) growth measures the earning per share growth from FY3 to FY5. ROE is the Return on Equity that measures the fund's annual return relative to total shareholders' equity. This ratio evaluates how quickly investments can be turned into profits. Long-term debt to capital measures a fund’s financial leverage by calculating the proportion of long-term debt used to finance its assets relative to the amount of equity used for the same purpose. A higher ratio indicates higher leverage. Operating margin measures the profit a fund makes for every dollar of sales after paying the variable expenses. Contribution to Return measures the performance impact from portfolio holdings over a defined time period. It takes into account both weight and performance of the portfolio holdings. Contribution to Return is calculated at security level.

data.title: GlobalFoundries: I'm Maintaining A Neutral View After The Secondary Offering
data.description:
Thesis

Today I’m taking another look at Globalfoundries Inc. (GFS), a semiconductor manufacturing company that focuses on specialized chips used for RF (radio frequency), power management, and photonics. The company operates semiconductor fabs in the United States, Europe, and Singapore.

In my previous coverage, I downgraded the stock from Buy to Hold because, although GlobalFoundries’ cash flow and profit margins were getting better, I thought that the stock price had already risen enough to reflect most of those improvements. At the same time, short-term growth for the company appeared likely to be slow, which reduced the potential upside.

Since my call, momentum mostly has been in the stock’s favor, but recently, it’s been in retreat, particularly after the company priced a secondary offering at $42. In light of that, after going over the most recent data, I'm maintaining a neutral position as the company’s improving profitability and exposure to AI-driven optical networking are, once again, largely reflected in the current valuation.

GlobalFoundries Capital Allocation and Business Momentum

Recently, GlobalFoundries set the price for a secondary share offering and at the same time announced that it will buy back some of its own shares. It’s a simple snapshot that shows how the company decides to use and manage its money. Mubadala Technology Investment Company's selling down part of its stake, while the company is using some balance sheet cash to repurchase shares under the new authorization. I also think what’s interesting is the timing of it.

This comes fairly soon after the quarterly results and that silicon photonics webinar with JPMorgan Chase, Wedbush and others. So, in a way, management's offering a glimpse of how their minds are working about the balance between returning cash to shareholders and reinvesting in the business.

The market showed some weakness, which is common when the supply of shares increases. However, this still fits with the company’s strong cash flow (while initially in decline, then edging up slightly), which comes from its focus on operations and improving productivity.

Now, to really understand why the company feels comfortable balancing shareholder returns with reinvestment, I think it helps to look at what the underlying business is actually doing. In the earnings discussion, the company explained that it improved profitability by clamping down on costs and shifting the mix of business it goes after. Revenue stayed about the same compared to the same period last year, but it increased compared to the previous quarter. The communications infrastructure and data center segments were the main drivers of this improvement, helped by strong demand in optical networking and satellite communications.

Much of the momentum traces back to a change in how AI data centers move data internally. As you probably already now by now, demand's being driven by AI data centers, that's where most of the traffic now moves between machines inside the data center (east-west traffic) rather than going in and out. But this large amount of machine-to-machine communication is revealing network bottlenecks, which is pushing companies to redesign their systems with more power-efficient connectivity. Because of this, customers are increasingly choosing optical technology to improve how efficiently their computing resources are used. According to management, this shift is a current architectural necessity, not exactly a long-term idea.

Getting ready for the transition, the company has been expanding both its manufacturing base and its tech ecosystem. The company also produced better results through steady operational execution. Recent acquisitions increased its manufacturing capacity in Singapore and added more design IP. These deals also expanded the company’s customer base and created synergies, helping it move faster on development plans in photonics and advanced packaging.

What's unique is that wider capability's already pulling the company deeper into work with major industry players. Specifically, the company said it's working closely with hyperscalers and major transceiver manufacturers. These efforts are supported by strong PDKs (process design kits) and partnerships with ecosystem companies that specialize in fiber coupling. Remember, GFS also manufactures products on both 200-millimeter wafers and 300-millimeter wafers, which gives production more resilience and flexibility. Essentially, its strategy focuses on technologies such as modulators, DWDM (dense wavelength division multiplexing), and heterogeneous integration. In a nutshell, these technologies are intended to solve latency and power issues in scale-up networks.

GlobalFoundries Industry Trends and Competitive Landscape

Recent developments in the sector I’ve looked at support this trend. The Optical Fiber Communication Conference 2026 outlook says that the rise of AI is driving a major shift toward optical networking technologies. And innovations such as co-packaged optics and higher-speed optical modules are becoming necessary to handle the rapidly growing east-west data traffic that moves between machines inside large hyperscale computing clusters. At the same time, predictions for the semiconductor industry show that the rising demand for AI infrastructure is changing both the types of chips that are needed and the number of chips required. Alongside this, onshoring pressures (efforts by countries to produce more technology within their own borders) are encouraging companies to spread their manufacturing across different regions. Taken together, this makes companies with facilities in multiple parts of the world, like GFS, with operations across several continents, better positioned to handle these changes.

Even so, it's fair to be cautiously skeptical about the risks involved in actually carrying out these plans. While management presents GFS as a leading company with a wide range of products, from pluggable optical modules to CPO (co-packaged optics), other competitors also say they hold strong market positions. In addition, moving to advanced packaging technologies is technically complicated and can create manufacturing yield and system integration challenges, which could slow down production increases in the near term.

Also, competition in SiGe (silicon germanium) technologies and power solutions used in data centers adds more pressure to the business, even though government partnerships and funding programs help the company use its capital more efficiently.

All of this ultimately comes down to the balance between opportunity and risk in how the company decides to use its cash. From that perspective, the secondary share sale and the accompanying share buyback aren't mainly about reacting to short-term movements in the market. Instead, they show a company trying to keep a tight grip on its capital.

Market Valuation and Growth Constraints

At the time of my analysis, GFS was trading ~$42. In context, that gives it a modest edge above a fair value estimate of ~$38, which is based on the company’s normalized earnings.

Meanwhile, its forward P/E of 22.61 factors in EPS compression toward 14.56 a couple of years out.

It seems like EV/EBITDA forward at 8.62 does look reasonable against sector peers, thanks to healthy cash flow and nearly $4 billion in liquidity. All in all, investors are clearly paying for these AI tailwinds and photonics upside despite middling to very poor growth grades, where, based on current forecasts, revenue's expected to grow about 5.58% going forward, and EBITDA are projected to grow only slightly above 4%.

Not much has change since I last covered the stock, in the sense that I still think the current valuation looks too high. The secondary share offering increases the number of shares available in the market just as the company plans to raise capex to about 15% to 20% of revenue to support packaging expansion and high-demand production areas (oversubscribed corridors). Although the company's also conducting a share repurchase, it only partly offsets the dilution risk from issuing more shares. Then there are the competitive and market risks. On top of that, GFS still has to deliver on CPO and deal with manufacturing yield problems, which raises the odds of downside if things don’t go as planned.

Final Verdict

All in all, there is no reason to upgrade back to that Buy rating I had on GFS last June, so I'm keeping it at a Hold. On the positive side, there is real potential growth from AI-driven photonics demand, along with benefits from diversified manufacturing locations that support onshoring efforts. On the risk side, it's still overvalued, the secondary share offering increases dilution while capex is rising.

Technically, we could see support hold here at the $40 level, but if it breaks, as a current GFS shareholder, I'd personally look to pick up some more shares closer to $35.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • US equities saw renewed volatility in the fourth quarter due to seemingly mixed investor sentiment toward AI valuations, economic uncertainties and key economic data being delayed by the government shutdown.
  • The initial third quarter GDP estimate showed robust growth of 4.3%, but labor market conditions softened with unemployment rising to 4.6%, a four-year high. Consumer spending remained resilient but showed early strain among lower income households.
  • Inflation moderated but stayed above the Fed’s 2% target, with the November CPI at 2.7%, which led the Fed to cut the federal funds rate twice in an effort to support growth amid easing inflation and labor market weakness.
  • Despite mid-quarter volatility, strong corporate earnings and Fed rate cuts helped the S&P 500 Index return 2.66% for the quarter. Health care and communication services sectors outperformed; real estate and utilities lagged.
  • Value stocks outperformed growth stocks. The Russell 1000 Value Index returned 3.81% compared to 1.12% for the Russell 1000 Growth Index.
  • Trends such as reduced policy uncertainty, the Fed’s monetary easing and softening economic data are expected to continue, with further rate cuts likely in 2026.
  • Though global growth may slow near term, healthy corporate balance sheets, labor market resilience and central bank easing should, in our view, limit sustained weakness, supporting a cautiously optimistic outlook for equities.

Compared to the Russell 1000 Index, the fund is generally balanced across sectors and industries, with exposure to areas that have been benefiting from long-term secular growth tailwinds, including e-commerce, electric vehicles, cloud computing, industrial automation, medical technology and broadband. Importantly, our dual focus on companies generating sustainable levels of free cash flow and having healthy balance sheets gives the fund a quality bias and provides defensive characteristics that we believe should prove valuable if market volatility persists.

Notable Additions

Linde (LIN) was purchased due to its essential and resilient business model, anchored by long-term contracts and leadership in industrial gases, which we believe positions it to benefit from megatrends like global energy transition and technology growth.

Abbott Laboratories (ABT) was added to the fund due to its diversified medical technology leadership, robust new product pipeline, including the upcoming Libre CGM sensor partnership with Medtronic (not a fund holding), and a favorable legal position in infant formula litigation. Despite near-term headwinds, we believe Abbott’s solid fundamentals and growth prospects position it well for long-term value.

Republic Services (RSG) is one of the largest US waste management companies and was added to the fund due to what we saw as its attractive risk/reward profile, solid execution and rising consensus estimates. With its consolidated industry structure, high barriers to entry, strong recurring revenues and compelling free cash flow yield, we believe Republic Services offers potential for stable growth, dividend increases and share repurchases, making it a defensive, value-oriented holding.

Notable Sales

Oracle (ORCL) is a major US technology company providing database software, cloud infrastructure and enterprise applications. We sold it due to concerns about downside risk as its investment in AI infrastructure has caused rising debt and negative free cash flow.

Stryker (SYK), a medical technology company, was sold because we believed the risk/reward profile had become less attractive as its valuation rose.

Smurfit Westrock (SW), a packaging company, was sold due to deteriorating fundamentals that have seemingly led to negative investor sentiment and uncertain timing for the stock to recover despite what we consider an attractive free cash flow yield.
