data.title: SPYI: A 12% Yield Derived From S&P Equities? Do Tell!
data.description:
My thesis today rests on the fact that the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) might not look like an ideal income vehicle despite the 11.8 percent TTM yield, but I would rate it a Buy for investors with a specific goal of generating tax-efficient returns in a market that has been moving sideways since November 2025, but also in a volatile manner. As a matter of fact, I would even go so far as to suggest that this fund can preserve one's capital in a novel fashion, so it affords that level of capital preservation. Not protection, mind you, but simply a unique way to offset capital gains, benefit from capital losses, and exploit market volatility.

The key to understanding this unique characteristic of SPYI is appreciate the true trade-off one makes in a market that may not be as hospitable to bullish investors as it has been the past three years.

To be clear, the trade-off I refer to is not that one accepts a cash flow in exchange for sacrificing significant equity upside. That is a given when a fund employs a call-selling strategy on equities it owns. The one I am trying to impress upon you is the complete lack of any downside protection in a declining or sideways market, particularly a volatile one like the present. This is what the true trade-off looks like when you put risk at the forefront of your investment decisions.

My intent with this analysis is to explore how SPYI fits into this puzzle, and who the right type of investor is for this income vehicle. Is it more suitable for capital preservation, is it intended to be tax-friendly, can it sustainably deliver positive outcomes for the investor, and so on and so forth. More importantly, how will it perform under what should treat as a largely unpredictable forward market climate?

These are the questions I intend to answer so novice investors can make better-informed decisions on whether or not to invest in cash-flow-generating ETFs such as SPYI.

Spoiler... my analysis will show the following:

  1. SPYI is a Buy for income investors seeking tax advantages, mainly via return-of-capital [ROC] distributions
  2. Yes, it helps with capital preservation, but not in a traditional sense; the "preservation" part comes from tax deferral on a declining cost basis
  3. Yes, the strategy with net-credit spreads has helped achieve near-parity with market performance on a total return basis, and in the current sideways but increasingly volatile market, the strategy should work even more efficiently
  4. Yes, I believe it can keep pace with the unpredictable market climate ahead of us, and it can manage (but not mitigate or protect from) drawdowns better than most other high-yield income funds because of the ROC component.

SPYI: Reading Between the Lines

The first priority is to see what the fund's objectives are:

The NEOS S&P 500® High Income ETF (the “Fund”) seeks to generate high monthly income in a tax efficient manner with the potential for equity appreciation in rising markets.

The phrases that stand out for me are "high monthly income", "tax efficient manner" and "equity appreciation". The qualifiers are "seeks to generate", "potential for", and "rising markets". I shall now put each of these in the context of a potential investment in SPYI.

"High Monthly Income" and "Tax Efficient Manner"

The core objective of this ETF, in my view, is to turn capital appreciation into a regular income stream, but without sacrificing too much upside and without creating a hefty tax burden on the investor. The fund does this in three ways:

  • use tax-friendly contracts on the derivatives side
  • return capital to investors for tax deferment purposes
  • offset equity gains with equity losses (tax loss harvesting)

Bear in mind that the fund holds equities but does not engage in options on those because that would create a taxable event. Instead, the fund enters into contracts on the S&P Index (SPX) on the call side. Utilizing a spread strategy involving writing calls on the SPX and using some of the proceeds to buy long OTM calls, the fund creates a net-credit situation that essentially powers a portion of the income generated by the fund. Since the contracts themselves are subject to a Section 1256 tax treatment, all capital gains (and losses) are taxed to the fund at a 60:40 ratio, 60 percent of it being long-term cap gains and 40 short-term.

This is not a benefit to the investor in the ETF, and this is where many investors tend to get confused. The benefit to the investor actually comes from the way distributions are accounted for, which is primarily via a return of capital (page 92).

One might incorrectly presume that a distribution comprising a large ROC component would erase that much of NAV and therefore erode one's capital, but that is, in fact, a major advantage from a taxation standpoint. This portion of one's monthly distribution does not immediately trigger a taxable event; rather, it reduces one's cost basis and defers the tax due on that payment.

To be perfectly clear, this is not tax-free, it is tax-deferred. The benefit to the investor comes from taxes being deferred on an increasingly dwindling cost basis. Of course, once your cost basis hits zero, any additional income will be taxed as ordinary income in the year it is received.

Since one's monthly income also comprises the dividends paid by the underlying S&P equities, some portion of this will be taxable as ordinary income. However, we saw that the fund also utilizes tax-loss harvesting as a method to offset equity gains, thereby limiting paper gains and further reducing the tax burden.

The "seeks to generate" qualifier is applicable here, specifically to the options spreads that are rolled over periodically to maintain the net credit position, and because it addresses the risk of the derivatives strategy being unable to fulfill its objective of generating tax-friendly income.

In order to understand this better, we must look at the equity portion of the fund - the bulk of its $8 billion AUM.

"Equity Appreciation"

This is the last of the highlighted phrases comprising the fund's objectives, and is crucial to total return. The whole point of investing in SPYI is to be able to indirectly hold a representative sample of the SP500 index's components and benefit from tax-friendly hard cash generated by the index options, rather than as on-paper appreciation or unrealized gains, and all the while keep bringing down your cost basis.

In a sense, it is a better way to hold a representative sample of the SP500 than with a normal index ETF like the (SPY). Equity appreciation occurs in both, of course, but we have seen the mechanics of how the tax burden on the investor would be lower with the SPYI option.

In terms of price appreciation, we shall now see something very interesting. What would seem to be gross underperformance is actually not that terrible.

A 68% underperformance on price does seem to be a pretty penny to pay for regular income in lieu of unrealized gains, but that delta is largely wiped out on a total return basis.

One would be correct to point out that the total return shown here involves reinvesting all distributions back into the ETF to achieve that level. The true total return would be even lower than the 62.7 percent we see above.

The real, material risk with SPYI is a broad market downturn. In such an event, the fund may still keep generating ROC distributions, but this will now directly eat into NAV because the offset will have been greatly diluted. There will be ample unrealized losses to harvest and possibly even carry over into the next reporting period, but the income from the net-credit spread will likely dry up. Long-term holders might recover their cash because their cost basis over time would be considerably lower than NAV, so anything over that is still extra.

I do not see this scenario happening. The market is wound up, certainly, and one sees that in the VIX chart at the end, but it is still holding steady despite the geopolitical upheaval impacting oil-dependent-but-not-self-sufficient nations and markets. But that is a discussion for another day.

Keep Your Personal Investment Goals in Full View

I shall reiterate my Buy rating for SPYI, but I would like to add some qualifiers.

Some investors would say that absolute total return is the only way to look at this, and from that perspective, SPYI does not cut the mustard.

Others with more of an income focus would disagree, arguing that this underperformance is a fair price to pay in exchange for an 11.8 percent yield that is also tax-friendly and keeps reducing the cost base.

A more extreme positive view would be to hold SPYI alone rather than any of the underlying equities or even the SPY.

All these views are equally valid, which is why one's own personal investment goals must necessarily be the focal point of this discussion. If you are an income investor looking to make money off the backs of hard-working and resilient SP500 equities, this might be the ideal fund for you. NEOS has a very wide range of high and enhanced income ETFs that layer derivatives on top of a wide range of asset types. Note the fact that many of these asset types are expected to appreciate over time - MLP and energy infra NAVs, real estate, the broad market indexes, and gold. Some are more stable, such as investment-grade corporate debt and government bills and bonds, while others are extremely volatile, like BTC-USD and (ETH-USD).

I shall be looking at several of these in the course of the following weeks, but my position on SPYI is that it is among the most likely to have its underlying assets appreciate over the long run. Equities typically do.

In case you were about to do a "gotcha - you forgot "potential for" and "rising markets"", I shall pre-empt it by saying that these two qualifiers are essential components of an SPYI investment case.

These qualifiers tell us two things: a) there are risks involved, which I discussed in the previous section, and b) a portion of that risk is based on the possibility that the market will not rise, thereby tempering one's equity appreciation expectations.

Interestingly, while I do see these risks as being material, it is nearly identical to the risk one would take with a pure equities holding anyway. In fact, it is quite possibly more prudent to hold SPYI over SPY because of this potential risk. Common sense informs us that SPY has zero protection of any kind, while SPYI not only manages to capture most of the SP500's upside, it also converts that captured upside into a regular stream of tax-friendly income for its investors. In a pinch, SPYI is more likely to return all of your capital than SPY.

The options layer is crucial here, of course, but a rising equity base feeds directly into that and is essential to offset any losses on the derivatives side, and vice versa. The downside risk may be fully or at least partially offset by the income generated and distributed, but that risk is magnified if the market moves down because the net-credit from the options positions will be fighting to offset an increasingly greater decline.

In summation, I am very comfortable rating SPYI a Buy. Volatility is great for a fund that utilizes this strategy, and with the VIX on the rise this year, we should hopefully see stronger distributions in the quarters ahead.

data.title: Zoetis: The King Of Animal Health On Sale
data.description:
Zoetis Inc. (ZTS) is a wide-moat company in a highly attractive industry, with significant opportunities of profitable growth going forward. However, the market is pricing it at below-average multiples, even lower than those of weaker competitors such as Elanco Animal Health Incorporated (ELAN). For these reasons we rate ZTS as a BUY, although there are risks, specifically related to the Librela and Solensia franchises, that should be closely monitored.

The Animal Health Industry is Highly Attractive

For those unfamiliar with the company, Zoetis is the global leader in animal health. The company operates in two segments, Companion Animals (dogs, cats, and horses), which is the most dynamic and accounts for 70% of revenue, while the remaining 30% corresponds to Livestock (cattle, swine, poultry, and fish), a more mature business and stable cash generator. ZTS is a global player with a presence in more than 100 countries. The firm has been in business since 1952, when Pfizer established its animal health division. In 2013, the company went public as a spin-off, and since then the share price has quadrupled, thanks to the company’s strong performance.

The animal health market is currently valued at approximately $50 billion, with an expected CAGR of 5-6%, reaching an estimated TAM of $90 billion by 2035. At first glance, this doesn’t seem spectacular. After all, the market size is just a fraction of human health, and its growth is far from sexier industries such as AI or semiconductors. Still, this industry possesses certain characteristics that make it highly attractive.

In first place, clients don’t have bargaining power. Unlike human healthcare, there are no public health systems, large insurers, or other intermediaries to exert downward pressure on prices. Instead, veterinarians and pet owners have little leverage against a highly concentrated industry. While this allows companies like Zoetis to consistently raise prices by 3–5% annually, as pricing power is ultimately capped by household budget constraints.

Second, there is a secular trend toward the humanization of pets. This means that pet owners treat their pets as family members. Several factors contribute to this phenomenon, such as delayed parenthood among Millennials and Gen Z, the rise of single-person households, and increased social isolation, to name just a few. Consequently, this stronger emotional connection has led to a higher number of pet owners, who are more committed to their pets’ quality of life, resulting in a larger TAM and lower income-elasticity.

According to Forbes, pet ownership in the US increased from 56% in 1988 to 66% in 2024, and 97% of pet owners consider their pets to be part of the family. Also, as shown in the following chart, US household expenditures on pets show a clear upward trend, rising from $507 in 2014 to $880 a decade later. Still, it is worth noting that while medical care is often the last thing owners cut back on, the overall rising cost of pet maintenance could lead some households to hesitate before adding new pets to the family, potentially cooling the long-term growth in ownership rates.

Third, a significant portion of revenue is highly predictable. Animals require monthly antiparasitic treatments, annual vaccines, and in cases of chronic illnesses such as osteoarthritis or atopic dermatitis, lifelong medication.

Fourth, the industry has high barriers to entry. Developing drugs requires years of testing and large investments that can only be recovered if the target market is large enough. Given that the animal health market is a fraction of the human healthcare, and the industry is highly concentrated, it is difficult for new companies to enter this space.

Finally, there is a trend toward higher animal protein consumption, especially in emerging economies. Consequently, sustained demand for livestock treatments is expected in the years to come.

Zoetis’ Wide Moat

Due to these characteristics, the industry looks very attractive to us, and Zoetis is the undisputed leader, holding a #1 or #2 position in almost every product category and geography where it competes. ZTS commands a market share of approximately 20% and has consistently gained share from its competitors over the last decade. In addition, this growth has been exceptionally profitable as the company has posted margins that rival those of top technology companies, making its SA profitability factor grade of A+ well deserved.

This outperformance is a result of Zoetis’ wide moat, which is sustained by several competitive advantages.

The company is an innovation powerhouse, with 5,500 granted patents and 1,600 pending applications across more than 50 countries. This portfolio is extremely difficult for competitors to replicate in a reasonable timeframe. Also, its R&D division is highly efficient, as demonstrated by the 17 blockbusters already launched and 12 pipeline candidates for the coming years, some of which target ‘white spaces’ with significant TAMs (CKD for example).

Unlike major rivals such as Merck Animal Health and Boehringer Ingelheim Animal Health, Zoetis is a pure-play animal health company. This focus allows ZTS for more independent capital allocation decisions, greater agility in M&A, and more efficiency in R&D and regulatory compliance.

The company possesses an R&D scale that other pure-play rivals simply cannot match. Zoetis invests just under $700 million in research annually, representing approximately 7-7.5% of revenue. While other pure-play competitors, such as Elanco allocate a similar proportion, this only amounts to half of Zoetis' spending in dollar terms. This additional financial capacity allows Zoetis to develop multiple therapeutic areas simultaneously, including frontier fields such as oncology, CKD, and cardiology. At the same time, having a diversified pipeline significantly reduces the inherent business risk of healthcare companies. That said, it is worth noting that Elanco is catching up in some areas and has made significant progress with its ‘Big 6’, and it is a competitor that is worth watching closely.

In addition, the firm benefits from strong brand recognition and an extensive distribution network. Also, pet owners trust their veterinarians, who recommend the products they are familiar with. Since veterinarians are not the ones ultimately paying for the medication, they tend to suggest premium brands like Zoetis. Despite this, recent concerns regarding the safety of Librela, which we will discuss later, constitute a blow to the company’s brand equity that must be monitored.

Further, since the Abaxis acquisition in 2018, Zoetis has been creating an ecosystem for veterinary clinics. While IDEXX Laboratories, Inc. (IDXX) remains the leader in diagnostics, ZTS’s point-of-care business grew by 14% in the US last year. This is significant not only because selling consumables is a highly profitable and stable cash flow stream, but also because veterinarians who use Zoetis’ blood analyzers are also more likely to purchase its medications. Simultaneously, the company gains more data to feed its drug development processes.

The Stock is Undervalued

Despite the attractive industry characteristics and the multiple competitive advantages that we described, the stock is trading at a depressed multiple. As shown in the following charts, ratios such as P/E and EV/EBITDA are at historically low levels. In fact, these multiples are below pandemic-era lows.

Zoetis' current forward P/E multiple is 17.15x, which is lower than the S&P 500 21.2x. Also, it is lower than its rival Elanco, which trades at 23.1x, despite Zoetis being a much higher quality company and outperforming ELAN in almost every metric.

Our intrinsic value estimate for Zoetis stock is based on a DCF model. Starting from a $2,283 million FCFF and $4,066 million EBITDA for 2025, we forecast their growth rate at 5.5% for the next ten years, which is the mid-point of the animal health market expected growth rate. Consequently, we assume no margin expansion or market share gains, and a constant cash conversion. We derive the terminal value from the one-year average of the Fwd EV/EBITDA multiple (16.52x) and the 2036 EBITDA estimate of $7,327 million. The 9% cost of capital was computed using the CAPM model. Based on these assumptions, we arrive at a $150 value for the Zoetis stock, which implies an upside potential of 25.2% from the current $120 stock price.

Apart from capital appreciation investors can get a quarterly dividend of $0.53 per share, which is a 1.77% dividend yield. At first glance the yield seems low, but it has been growing at double-digit rates (approximately 15%). In our view this trend will continue; because as growth converges toward a terminal rate, a lower required reinvestment rate should allow management to return more cash to shareholders. A Seeking Alpha Dividend Safety Grade of 'B' indicates that the dividend is very safe.

Zoetis’s also rewards shareholders via share buybacks. Historically, its share buyback rate has been around 1-2% but it increased significantly following the price drops in November last year. This is a very positive signal as it signals that management believes the stock is undervalued.

Risks to the Thesis

While top line growth slowed from 8.3% in 2024 to 2.3% last year, we believe the main reason behind the stock’s compressed valuation is the controversy surrounding Librela, a canine osteoarthritis pain treatment, and Solensia, its equivalent for cats.

This was one of the most successful launches in Zoetis’s history, reaching $400 million in revenue in its first year, but since 2024 pet owners have shared anecdotal evidence on social media linking the drug to adverse effects such as cognitive impairment, and urinary and gastrointestinal issues.

The company defends the safety profile of these drugs, indicating that the incidence rate is very rare (less than 0.1%), and that 80% of reports involve geriatric pets. Under these circumstances, it is difficult to distinguish drug effects from the natural progression of pre-existing conditions. Also, the firm argues that for most pets with chronic pain, Librela offers an improvement in quality of life that outweighs its potential risks.

Beyond these arguments, the reality is that real-world results have differed from initial studies, leading the company to implement significant changes in product labeling. In addition, some clinics have suspended the administration of Librela and Solensia until they have more visibility, and sales of these products declined in 2025. While studies like the Global Pharmacovigilance report indicate a very low incidence, and others suggest the risk-benefit ratio remains positive for cases without pre-existing conditions, Zoetis’ reputation and brand equity have taken a hit.

In this regard, investors should remain alert to FDA post-marketing surveillance studies, with results expected in H1 2026. An adverse outcome could jeopardize not only the Librela franchise, but also Lenivia, a new monoclonal antibody for osteoarthritis with slightly different chemistry, and a once-every-three-months application.

We remain cautiously optimistic about management navigating this crisis due to its clean track record, its openness in handling the controversy, and the confidence they have in the drugs. However, uncertainty will remain high in the coming months, and this risk cannot be ignored by investors.

Another risk is connected to food safety in the Livestock segment. A defective product supplied to livestock that makes its way through the human food supply chain could trigger a public health crisis. Such an event would not only lead to massive litigation by FDA or EFSA, but would also impair trust from producers, who are facing increasingly strict safety regulations.

Regarding competition, there is a possibility that Elanco’s Zenrelia could erode the Apoquel/Cytopoint dermatology franchises more than expected in 2026.

Another risk for Zoetis, as it is for most companies, is an economic slowdown or recession. Such an event could contract pet healthcare spending for consumers with tight budgets, as seen during the GFC. While we believe the decline should be less severe than in other consumer categories, non-critical treatments could be postponed.

In terms of long-term growth, there is a possibility that declining birth rates, even below the replacement rate in some countries, could limit the growth rate of the pet population. While not an extreme risk, fewer households could translate into fewer pets as well.

We plan to initiate a small tracking position in Zoetis rather than a full position while the uncertainty surrounding the Librela and Solensia franchises persists. That said, any clarity regarding these safety issues would likely act as the necessary catalyst for the stock to close the gap with our valuation.

data.title: CarMax: Starboard Value's Letter Highlights Important Topics
data.description:
The CarMax Investment Thesis

CarMax, Inc. (KMX) now has Starboard Value, a hedge fund known for its activist campaigns, as an investor. And Starboard Value already has experience in the automotive industry from its engagement with Cars.com (CARS) in 2017. I also see the points proposed by Starboard Value as goal-oriented, which is why I think there is a good chance that CarMax could develop positively.

But as I generally consider the industry to be rather unattractive and turnarounds are always difficult, I will remain with my Hold rating for the time being.

What was my previous coverage like?

In my last article about CarMax, I explained that I don't see any significant competitive advantages for the company. In particular, I found the capital efficiency and the ROIC not worth investing in. But this is also because some industries are simply less attractive for investments. And when the management team suggested that they wanted to reduce the margins to be more competitive. CarMax was completely out of the question for me. A company that already has low margins and then wants to engage in a price war is usually not a good investment.

But with Starboard Value's involvement, things might change. That's why I'd like to take a look at Starboard Value's suggestions to see if they change my mind.

Starboard Value's ideas

Starboard Value has a stake of around $350m in CarMax, which currently has a market cap of $6b. At the next Shareholder Meeting, Starboard Value will also nominate two members for the CarMax board to support the change process. But what suggestions has Starboard Value already made in its letter? Let's take a look at that now.

Near-term improvements in reconditioning efficiency and prioritization can directly reduce cost of goods sold and help offset pricing adjustments on the selling side.

Source: Starboard Value Letter to KMX.

Starboard Value sees potential for cost savings that could help to mitigate the impact of the price war on margins. If this were to succeed and CarMax were to increase its market share at the same time, this would be a positive sign for the future. It is particularly important to win back market share, as the Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index is rising slightly while CarMax is simultaneously experiencing volume declines.

On the selling side, we believe the core issue is execution rather than brand strength or value proposition.

Source: Starboard Value Letter to KMX.

I can only fully agree with that. The CarMax brand is still strong, but the execution of the last few years has simply not been optimal. I think the physical footprint could have been used more effectively and the focus should have been on online earlier. The online buying and selling process should be as simple as possible for CarMax customers. The fewer unnecessary steps there are, the fewer people will drop out during the process.

But even if the transition to online was somewhat delayed, there is now a new chance with AI. With the help of AI, it will be possible to streamline the company's structure and optimize workflows. I think CarMax should take an aggressive approach here, as the company that leverages AI the best will probably have the best margins in the end. With the AI-powered search engine, CarMax is already taking a promising first step.

Figuring out how to combine online and AI will probably be the most important factor in the industry over the next few years.

We believe modest price reductions (which may be temporary based on higher priced purchased inventory) of approximately $100 to $300 per vehicle and combined with a more responsive, data-driven pricing system that adjusts in real time to local market conditions can restore competitiveness.

Source: Starboard Value Letter to KMX.

Using AI and the data that CarMax has on the car market, it should be possible to develop a pricing system that could give the company a competitive advantage. There are probably few companies that can match CarMax's data quality. So CarMax should definitely try to use it.

Overall, I believe that Starboard Value's suggestions are definitely heading in the right direction and will contribute to the turnaround. But a turnaround is always difficult and success is not guaranteed.

What else has happened regarding CarMax?

Giverny Capital Asset Management has exited its position in CarMax. But this doesn't surprise me, as I had often wondered before why they even had a stake at all. As Akre Capital Management has also significantly reduced its stake, the two investors who brought CarMax to my attention through their investment are no longer significantly invested.

Valuation

The ROICs of CarMax and some of its closest competitors clearly show that the industry is characterized by very low ROICs. This alone is a deal-breaker for me, as the ROIC-WACC spreads of some of the companies are probably even negative. This means that the investments destroy value rather than creating it.

This means that I currently don't see any multiple upside potential for CarMax. If CarMax can increase its ROIC again, I could see a higher multiple. But this would require a successful turnaround first. So, right now, I see CarMax as fairly valued for a company that is at a point of transition.

Risks

Although I like the ideas of Starboard Value, there is still a long way to go between ideas and implementation. Ultimately, execution is key. And even if you have the best ideas, a unattractive industry can still be problematic.

Conclusion

In my opinion, the new CEO and Starboard Value's involvement are positive aspects for CarMax shareholders. I think that this will prompt an attempt to successfully turn things around. No one can say for sure if it will work out, but I think the ideas are moving in the right direction. But I think that within the next year it will become clear whether there is a positive impact on costs and how the market share develops.

data.title: SBA Communications: Near-Term AFFO Pressure, But Long-Term Recovery Potential Remains
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered SBA Communications (SBAC), I highlighted their attractive valuation, portfolio rebalancing moves, and relatively high levels of leverage that they're working on addressing, with potential for better shareholder returns.

Following a report in line with my expectations, SBAC’s valuation remains relatively attractive given the industry we’re talking about, with solid recovery potential after some near-term pressure.

Internal Developments

SBAC reported an okay Q4 and 2025 as a whole, with an FFO in line with the market’s estimates but a slight miss on revenue, with the AFFO falling to $1.38 billion, or about $12.85 per share, mostly as a result of a mix of pressure caused by the weak environment, the churn seen in the industry, higher interest expenses and costs, as well as the DISH (SATS) issues mentioned before.

Regarding DISH, I’ll also mention that the impact here is not as big as the other players since we’re talking about shorter-term contracts, with SBAC recently filing a lawsuit to protect their ~$100 million total exposure, as the CFO mentioned during the recent Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026:

DISH was starting a fourth carrier in the U.S. They needed basically towers to deploy their equipment. We signed agreements with them, lease agreement. It's about $56 million of annual revenues. Last year, we did $37 million lease of $2 million aware with DISH. Almost nothing in the second half. They stopped paying. We have basically a $56 million revenue contracted with DISH in 2026. We assume it's going to be a 100% churn for 2026. We have short-term contract with DISH, the total exposure under the term of the leases that we have is slightly above $100 million. They run out through at the end of '27 and '28. Our total exposure is about $100 million, and we filed a lawsuit, basically, in order to protect our legal rights.

Regarding the guidance, SBAC sees an AFFO of about $1.26 billion to $1.30 billion in 2026 ($11.80 to $12.29 per share), meaning another drop due to the ongoing pressure seen both domestically and internationally.

Given that fact that, as they mention, this is the last big year of Sprint churn (less than $20 million of that left over the next couple of years) and also the DISH hit and the one from the international operations, with the significant recent acquisitions being integrated, plus massive maturities in 2026 that will have to be refinanced at likely higher rates, we're likely looking at a turnaround year (or two) in terms of AFFO, especially if macro conditions improve and debt costs continue to fall.

Financially, based on SBAC’s latest report, we continue to see what I’d call a weak position, although that's not really uncommon for this industry, with their current assets being well below the current liabilities and a lot of debt and leverage.

As they mention, their weighted average interest currently stands at 3.8%, but there are significant ~$1.92 billion in maturities coming in 2026, which have interest rates of only 1.884% (January; paid off) and 1.631% (November; expected to be refinanced at 5.25%, as per the CFO), adding a lot of interest once refinanced, while 2027 comes with over $3 billion to be refinanced, which is why I believe we should expect pressure for a couple of years.

SBAC also announced increasing their dividend by 12.6% recently, to $1.25 per quarter, representing about $533.25 million per year (after the boost), for a payout of about 38.6% based on last year’s AFFO. However, we see them returning a similar amount, $497.8 million in buybacks (with $1.1 billion left under the authorization), which were ramped up significantly alongside acquisitions, while the leverage remained relatively high but not uncommon for a REIT like this.

Macro Developments

Although we’re talking about a REIT with global operations (especially in the Americas), the ongoing Iran conflict can still have significant effects, as we’re talking about something that can affect the global economy, going far beyond the significant jump in oil, gas or fertilizers we can see in recent days since the conflict escalated.

Given the fact that Iran is a giant natural fortress and religious beliefs will make a regime change realistically impossible - as mentioned in more detail in previous articles - I don’t expect this to be the goal, meaning that we might see it de-escalate following a “quick skirmish,” which also seems to be the case given Trump’s recent statements, although Iran said the opposite.

If not, then the consequences for the broader economy can be really significant, as we’re talking about a conflict that has detrimental effects on global markets from several points of view, from significant supply chain disruptions or higher shipping costs, to inflation and even worse, all at a terrible time for a global economy that was just recently showing some signs of improvement following the rate hikes, political uncertainty, tariffs, etc. that caused significant slowdowns.

Especially since we’re talking about a REIT in this industry, any bit of slowdowns or even a higher-for-longer rate environment can have significant effects on them and their tenants, who are notorious for their tens or even hundreds of billions in debt.

There were some signs of improvement in the broader economy before this, but it remains something that really can’t be ignored, while the potential Japan US treasury sell-off can also put significant pressure on an already weakened economy, as it can inflate the supply and pressure these yields, as capital can migrate back into its domestic market since the opportunity cost improves.

Although the ongoing conflict is “stealing the spotlight,” I really wouldn’t ignore the situation in Japan, and as we can see, the 10-year bond yields grew significantly in recent years, from virtually nothing to over 2% now. Japan’s recent economic plans and them doing pretty much the opposite of what the rest of the world is doing (i.e. hiking rates when others are cutting) make these yields more attractive, and if these directions continue, they can certainly put a lot of pressure on the borrowing costs of not only the US itself, but also those of companies like SBAC or their clients, which rely on rolling their debt over and over.

Especially since recent statements from the BoJ indicate that rate hikes are likely to continue and the country's debt is seen rising by 28% until 2029, this situation is certainly not over.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, regardless of how defensive the infrastructure that this company owns may be, SBAC and its clients will always depend on broader economic conditions, which can also be seen in their valuation.

Given the ongoing geopolitical tensions and risk, it’s certainly possible to see the company’s valuation remain attractive, while a higher-for-longer rate environment can directly impact their refinancing activity, resulting in higher levels of interest, while their customers could also slow down, consolidate even more, or even exit certain markets in an effort to reduce costs and adapt, putting even more pressure on the company.

Valuation

For SBAC’s DCF to Equity valuation, starting from the midpoint AFFO of $1.28 billion they expect in 2026, I’ll assume the effects of their refinancing and potential for more churn, getting us to a conservative $1.1 billion in 2027.

Assuming a recovery from this point as we could also see a potential recovery both domestically and internationally, assuming they’ll grow at a CAGR of 6.5% until 2030 given their acquisitions and potential buybacks boosting the per share amounts, followed by a CAGR of 3% for the subsequent 5 years, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate, a discount rate of 8.5%, as well as 106.65 million shares outstanding.

This way, we get to an equity value of $20.24 billion, for an intrinsic value of $189.82 per share, which is above the current $183.48.

Conclusions

I’ll reiterate SBAC’s Buy rating, as the valuation remains relatively attractive given the industry, with potential for a turnaround and better shareholder returns in the coming years.

There are certainly risks given the churn, economic weakness, or debt, but the current levels already take into account this potential weakness to some degree, with solid long-term potential.

data.title: IBIT: Navigating The Deep Correction Toward A Long-Term $126,000 Target
data.description:
Investment thesis

For crypto investors who believe in Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) growth potential, the start of 2026 has not been what they expected. Over the past 5 months, there has been a negative change in the price of the leading cryptocurrency, matching the record bear market of 2018-2019. Consequently, the dynamics of spot ETFs on Bitcoin, where the "gold standard" is the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT), is also negatively affected. The fundamental background remains negative, with the situation in Iran showing no signs of de-escalation, Bitcoin's price may attempt to continue its decline to complete a deep correction to the $50,000–$55,000 price zone. Nevertheless, my long-term objectives remain unchanged, with a renewed all-time high of $126,223. At the same time, it is not certain that the market has not already reversed, finding its cyclical bottom at $60,000. If we assume that the current price of Bitcoin at $69,000 will be the entry point for IBIT, the potential risk/reward ratio is 1 to 4, in my opinion, a cost-effective bet on the fundamental growth of the cryptocurrency's value. Given this, my rating for IBIT remains at "Buy", because the current price of Bitcoin provides a favorable entry point into the market with a high risk-reward ratio.

My Previous Thesis and Main Arguments

I compared this fund with its main competitor among option players, NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BTCI). The main point was that if the market moves into a phase of Bitcoin recovery growth, then IBIT will do better than the BTCI options mix, where some of the profits get cut off when the price goes up aggressively. In addition, earlier I believed that in 2026 we would see a potential renewal of Bitcoin's historical high, reaching a price range of $130,000-$150,000. The actual results turned out to be different, and since then, IBIT has shown a negative return of -11.57%. This downward movement in the cryptocurrency market is mainly driven by the potential change in the Fed's rhetoric towards tightening monetary policy (the Warsh effect) as well as a general change in investor risk appetite, reflects market participants' concerns and fears about the future fate of any risky assets (attributable to increased volatility in stock markets and the VIX index).

IBIT Overview and Capital Dynamics

IBIT is the largest Bitcoin fund, the mechanism of which is based on physical ownership of cryptocurrency, whereby each share is backed by a specific number of tokens held in custody (this service is provided by Coinbase Custody using hardware wallets). Under BlackRock's management is $52.77 billion in investor capital, exceeding the combined total of its four closest competitors ($28.52 billion). Its commission is 0.25%, lower than that of the Gryscale Bitcoin Trust (GBTC) ETF, yet equal to that of the Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund ETF (FBTC).

Bitcoin price is the main catalyst for IBIT growth, affecting the dynamics of customer inflows and outflows. AUM has seen positive growth over the past year, with inflows of $22.98 billion, just as it did over the past six months ($3.81 billion), three months ($59.32 million), and last month ($824.56 million). Nevertheless, from mid-October, the process slowed significantly, demonstrating a greater outflow of funds. The situation only changed at the end of February, and inflows began to prevail. The inflow of funds over the last 5 days alone amounted to $770.8 million.

The inflow of capital from institutional investors is an important condition for increasing the attractiveness of IBIT. Dynamics of inflows and outflows from this type of client depend on a stable environment, which requires the adoption of all principles of the Genius Act. Based on the latest news, President Donald Trump has criticized American banks for slowing down the adoption of major regulatory changes in the cryptocurrency sector. Banks and Wall Street reps say this law is a threat because it could cause clients to move their money out of traditional bank accounts and deposits, and that could slow down lending to the real economy. The bill has been stalled since the January meeting of the Senate Banking Committee, with proposed amendments calling for the elimination of fees for stablecoin transactions.

The dynamics of institutional investor inflows and outflows prove that this law affects IBIT's growth potential and Bitcoin prices. During the past 12 months, a total of 1,595 institutional investors joined IBIT, whereas 527 institutional investors withdrew funds. There was a net inflow of 1,068 institutional investors with large capital, with a net inflow of $5.36 billion. Major capital inflows into IBIT were still seen in Q4 2024, whereas Q4 2025 saw the largest outflow of funds, at $968 million. Net institutional capital inflows, though, remained positive at $1.25 billion.

Technical Analysis of Bitcoin

Because of the direct correlation between IBIT's price and Bitcoin, to figure out the fund's price situation, you should check out the BTC-USD chart. Prices are moving within an aggressive downward trend channel, encountering a general support zone of $49,577–$60,760. The lower part of this range, I believe, will be more crucial in preventing prices from falling further. At the same time, earlier the support zone was $74,434–$78,197, and in the beginning of February it was broken, acting as resistance now. It is only when the price returns above this level, that will confirm the exit from the trend channel, will the reversal of Bitcoin from the phase of deep correction to recovery growth be confirmed.

On a monthly timeframe, though, Bitcoin's looking more interesting, telling us that the price has hit the discount zone between the 0.50 and 0.618 Fibonacci levels, spread across the asset's global cycles. The price zone between $57,802 and $70,876 is of interest for long-term purchases, coinciding with the token's current price situation. Additionally, based on current prices, Bitcoin is trading at its 200-week moving average on the 1W chart, providing support and contributing to a potential technical rebound.

Justification for the "Buy" Rating for IBIT through Technical Analysis of Bitcoin

The rationale for IBIT's "Buy" rating is related to the revision of the main target price, earlier set in the range of $130,000–$150,000, and now reduced to $126,000. This is due to a shift in strategy, from one that was previously based on speculative growth to one that is now more realistically based on a model of retesting the cyclical high. The level coincides with the high recorded in October 2025 and now represents a large pool of liquidity. Although the same level of $74,000 may prove to be a serious obstacle, if the weekly candle closes confidently above it, the bearish thesis (which, I believe, had a target of $50,000) shall be completely invalidated.

Furthermore, a resistance level of $74,000 has become a trigger for a potential short squeeze, since it is backed by a significant volume of sellers' stop losses. A price break above this level could trigger these stops automatically, further fueling Bitcoin's growth. What's more, the former historical high (2024) is located right here, with many long-term holders still stuck in their positions. The pressure on sellers is driven by their desire to break even, and this explains the current stalemate. Furthermore, over the past two weeks (February 23 to March 8), inflows of $1.355 billion into spot Bitcoin ETFs were recorded in this current price zone, indicating investors' intention to buy up price drops below $70,000. As a result, there is a strong accumulation zone forming here, acting as support and a foundation for future growth.

Justification for the "Buy" Rating for IBIT through Fundamental Analysis of Bitcoin

Analyzing the fundamental factors for Bitcoin's growth with the aim of retesting the historic level of 126,000, we should consider the macroeconomic reversal observed through the global M2 indicator. This digital token has always been a barometer of liquidity, going up in price when global liquidity grows. While there are some risks for the Fed's monetary policy, rising government debt and the need to service it will cause money supply to expand in 2026. Correlation between Bitcoin and global liquidity has always been high, which is why a 10-15% increase in global liquidity could cause cryptocurrency to rise again to its historic peak. Only in 2025, growth in global liquidity amounted to $9.49 trillion, or 10.71%. Looking at the previous bear cycle in 2022-2023, on the contrary, a decline in global M2 liquidity preceded it. This is why the current divergence has signaled that Bitcoin is expected to resume growth following the index.

It is also worth considering the fundamental factor of regulatory arbitrage surrounding the adoption of the Genius Act, which is not a setback but a sign of a compressed spring that will lead to Bitcoin's growth. Legalization of the law will create a legitimate gateway for banking capital. Opposition from the lobbying efforts of the largest US banks proves the high practical significance of the law, because it creates competition for customer funds. It is inevitable that a compromise will be found (as is typical for US legislation), enabling Bitcoin to transition to the status of a strategic reserve asset. Furthermore, Polymarket's odds that this law will be passed in 2026 are currently above 50%. If the Genius Act is passed, this will allow institutional investors to allocate capital for the purchase of digital tokens, generating exponential demand for them despite low liquidity on the exchanges themselves. Right now, the number of Bitcoins on crypto exchanges is the lowest it's been in years, a sign that whales who believe in its future growth are holding on to them.

Moreover, the ETF accounts for only 6% of the total volume of Bitcoin in circulation, thus reducing market volatility during downturns, because institutional investors buy up price drops. Additionally, an increasing number of companies are considering holding cryptocurrency in their accounts, driving up the price. One should also be aware of the significant reduction in daily Bitcoin issuance, whilst net inflows into ETFs such as IBIT continue to grow, resulting in long-term demand exceeding the volume of tokens mined, thus creating an imbalance between supply and demand. Furthermore, Bitcoin's mining cycle is declining and gradually becoming smaller, accounting for approximately 14,000 tokens per month (that is how many new coins enter the market).

Risks to the Investment Thesis due to Fundamental Factors

Currently, purchases of IBIT and Bitcoin are taking place amid a "perfect storm," suggesting a number of fundamental risks that could prevent the scenario of growth to $126,000 from materializing.

Firstly, a scenario in which the Fed moves to tighten monetary policy is becoming increasingly likely, not only due to the "Warsh effect", as well as increased inflationary risks (owing to rising oil and gas prices caused by the escalation of the conflict in Iran). If this happens, it will lead to both higher borrowing costs, resulting in reduced market liquidity and lower investor appetite for risky assets, but also a stronger US dollar, directly putting pressure on cryptocurrency prices.

Secondly, the banks' resistance to the Genius Act poses a regulatory risk. There may be significant amendments to the bill, preventing the full legalization of stablecoins and simplifying the entry of institutional capital. Lobbying is likely to come from banks, citing threats to their deposit outflows, the potential for which, in their view, could lead to a banking crisis in the US economy. The bill is likely to remain frozen until the end of 2026, and a significant outflow of institutional capital already invested in IBIT is possible.

Thirdly, one should not ignore the potential increase in geopolitical risks if there is no de-escalation in Iran. Under these circumstances, risk appetite among investors will not return, oil prices will not fall back to their original levels, which will heighten the threat of inflation and tightening of the Fed's monetary policy. The result would be an outflow of liquidity from the cryptocurrency market, impacting the price of Bitcoin and IBIT's AUM.

Conclusion

There is no doubt that the current decline in Bitcoin's price is a sign of a protracted correction reminiscent of the bearish cycle of 2018-2019. The fundamental basis of that period cannot be compared to the current one, though, since the cryptocurrency market has undergone a long period of transformation and improvement. Right now, the price of Bitcoin is in a "discount" zone, so based on technical analysis and Fibonacci levels, there’s a good chance to get into the market by buying the IBIT spot ETF. Even though a bunch of fundamental risk factors could push the technical correction to around $50,000–$55,000, long-term growth potential is limited, however, by the historical high of $126,000. The 1:4 risk-reward ratio therefore makes it an economically viable trade to bet on Bitcoin price growth through the purchase of IBIT. Nevertheless, we must bear in mind that the current price environment for cryptocurrency assets depends not on the adoption of blockchain technology and concepts, yet on psychological factors that take into account events in global politics and the US economy
